title: Worker message handler falls through to the full ORF pipeline branch for any unknown message type, including future protocol extensions.
description: The message handler checks `type === 'reprocess_live'` and `type === 'reprocess_thumb_live'` explicitly, then falls through to the full pipeline branch which destructures `{ id, bytes, options }`. If a new message type is added in the future (e.g., `type: 'cancel'` or `type: 'ping'`), it will be silently treated as a pipeline task with `bytes = undefined`, causing `process_orf(undefined, ...)` to throw a WASM error. An explicit `else` clause with an unknown-type guard would make the protocol closed.

title: take_rgb() is called once on result, but fullRgb is then passed to both downscale_rgb AND rgb_to_rgba — the buffer may be invalidated after the first WASM call.
description: `take_rgb()` moves the RGB buffer out of WASM memory and returns a `Uint8Array` slice. The generated binding slices from `getUint8ArrayMemory0()` and immediately frees the WASM-side memory. The returned `v1` is a `.slice()` copy (line 178 in the generated JS), so it is a detached JS-owned copy. Both subsequent calls (`downscale_rgb(fullRgb, ...)` and `rgb_to_rgba(fullRgb)`) pass this same `fullRgb` array into WASM via `passArray8ToWasm0`, which copies it into WASM memory each time. This is safe as currently generated, but the pattern depends on `.slice()` behavior in the generated binding — if the binding ever changes to a view instead of a copy, both calls would share WASM memory and the second call after `downscale_rgb` frees the allocation would read freed memory.

title: wb_mode 0xFFFF sentinel is correctly translated to null, but its meaning as 'absent' is not documented at the use site.
description: The worker correctly handles `result.wb_mode === 0xFFFF ? null : result.wb_mode` (line 179), translating the Rust sentinel to JS null. The main thread's `fmtWb` function (main.js line 1027) uses `exif.wbMode != null` before looking up `OLY_WB_MODE[exif.wbMode]`. This chain is correct. However, the sentinel value 0xFFFF is a magic number defined only in the Rust source; if the Rust side changes the sentinel, the JS null-conversion would silently become a visible numeric value in the UI. The contract should be documented with a constant name.

title: Rational sentinel 0/0 for exposure and fnumber is correctly guarded by denominator > 0 check in the worker.
description: Lines 170-174 check `result.exposure_den > 0` and `result.fnumber_den > 0` before constructing the rational objects, correctly handling the 0/0 absent sentinel. The main thread's `fmtShutter` and `fmtFNumber` also check `!rat || !rat.d` before dividing. The full chain is correct. One edge case: if `exposure_den` is 0 but `exposure_num` is non-zero (a malformed EXIF value), the rational is discarded entirely rather than reporting an error — this is intentional but could mask corrupt EXIF.
code_excerpt:
exposure:   result.exposure_den   > 0 ? { n: result.exposure_num,   d: result.exposure_den   } : null,
fnumber:    result.fnumber_den    > 0 ? { n: result.fnumber_num,    d: result.fnumber_den    } : null,
focalLength: result.focal_length_den > 0 ? { n: result.focal_length_num, d: result.focal_length_den } : null,

title: Pool _onMessage does not handle 'error_live' type for the lightbox — it invokes _liveHandler but the handler at line 402 only checks for 'lightbox_live'.
description: When the worker posts `{ type: 'error_live', error: 'no live state' }`, `_onMessage` routes it to `this._liveHandler(ev.data)`. The live handler at line 402 sets `liveInFlight = false` and then checks `if (msg.type === 'lightbox_live' && ...)`. For an 'error_live' message, this condition is false, the canvas is not updated, `liveInFlight` is correctly reset, and `livePendingLook` is drained. So the state machine recovers correctly, but the error is silently ignored — no console log, no user feedback. Users would see the slider unresponsive without knowing why.

title: color_matrix_used() is called after take_rgb() — the ProcessResult may be partially consumed, and field access order is undocumented.
description: `result.take_rgb()` moves the RGB buffer out of the Rust struct. The Rust implementation presumably sets the internal Vec to empty after `take_rgb`. Subsequent access to `result.color_matrix_used()` (line 163, called before `take_rgb16_lb` and `take_rgb16_thumb`) relies on the color matrix being a separate field unaffected by the take. This is correct as implemented, but the safe access order is nowhere documented. If Rust refactors the struct to lazily compute color_matrix from the rgb16 buffer, calling it after `take_rgb16_lb` would fail.
code_excerpt:
const fullRgb = result.take_rgb();           // line 149 - moves rgb out
// ...
const colorMatrix = new Float32Array(result.color_matrix_used());  // line 163
const lb16 = result.take_rgb16_lb();         // line 186
const thumb16 = result.take_rgb16_thumb();   // line 192
